coredata.dc:title: Inactivation of Microcystis aeruginosa and degradation of microcystin by urea-derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Tianjiao
Cheng, Bingxu
Yue, Le
Cao, Xuesong
Wang, Chuanxi
Wang, Zhenyu
Xing, Baoshan
coredata.dc:description: Harmful algal blooms and microcystin (MC-LR) are threats to fresh aquatic environments and even human health. Herein, the urea-derived carbon dots (UDs) prepared by hydrothermal method have the small size and good fluorescence properties. Compared with nitrate nitrogen and control, UDs (N content: 10 mg/L) could significantly inhibit the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa, with a growth inhibition rate of 78.6 %. Furthermore, UDs could also degrade MC-LR, achieving a degradation rate of 97.7 % after 96 h of illumination. The hetero-aggregation between UDs and algae on the surface of algal cells can block the absorption and utilization of light energy by algal cells, thereby reducing the synthesis of chlorophyll. UDs can also serve as a photocatalyst for the generation of free radicals (O2 −, ·OH), leading to cell membrane damage and inhibition of algal cell growth. Besides, O2 − and ·OH are capable of attacking the C4-C5 and C6-C7 bonds present in Adda-amino acids, subsequently facilitating the degradation of phycotoxins, ultimately resulting in their decomposition. These findings provide new insights into the application of urea-derived carbon dots in controlling harmful algal blooms and reducing MC-LR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Harmful algal blooms control
Urea-derived carbon dots
Hetero-aggregation
Free radicals
Microcystin degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of photocatalytic properties of UDs
Effects of UDs on the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa cells
The inhibitory mechanism of UDs on algal growth
Photocatalytic degradation of microcystin by UDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of UDs. (a) Flowchart for preparing a UDs, (b) TEM images of UDs, (c) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum of UDs, (d) FTIR spectroscopy of UDs, High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (e) and N1s (f) peaks of UDs, (g) Fluorescence lifetime of UDs.

Effects of UDs on growth of Microcystis aeruginosa algal cells. (a) Algae density of Microcystis aeruginosa algal cells exposed to different N sources (N content: 247.1 mg/L), (b) Growth rate of Microcystis aeruginosa algal cells exposed to different N sources, (c) Changes in Microcystis aeruginosa algal density after exposure to UDs (N content: 1, 5, and 10 mg/L), (d) Changes in the growth inhibition rate of Microcystis aeruginosa algal cells exposure to UDs (N content: 1, 5, and 10 mg/L).

The inhibitory mechanism of UDs on algal growth. (a) Inhibition ratio of algal cell fat-soluble chlorophyll and phycobilin content after exposure to UDs (N content: 1, 5, and 10 mg/L) at ambient concentration, (b) Laser confocal images of algal cell membrane damage levels (right) and oxidative stress levels (left), (c) SEM images of Microcystis aeruginosa upon exposure to UDs, (d) Intensity of O2 −, ·OH and h + in algal cell culture medium.
The mechanism of UDs inducing membrane damage through photocatalysis.

The UDs photocatalytic degradation of MC-LR. (a) Absorbance change of algal cell medium at 264 nm, (b) The intracellular and extracellular MC-LR content of Microcystis aeruginosa after exposure to UDs, (c) Contribution rate of O2 −, ·OH and h + to microcystin degradation after 96 h illumination.
Two possible pathways for MC-LR photodegradation by UDs.

head.ce:title: Inactivation of Microcystis aeruginosa and degradation of microcystin by urea-derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Processes and Pollution Control, and School of Environment and Ecology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Processes and Pollution Control, and School of Environment and Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Processes and Pollution Control, and School of Environment and Ecology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Stockbridge School of Agriculture, University of Massachusetts, Amherst, MA 01003, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Stockbridge School of Agriculture, University of Massachusetts, Amherst, MA 01003, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Harmful algal blooms and microcystin (MC-LR) are threats to fresh aquatic environments and even human health. Herein, the urea-derived carbon dots (UDs) prepared by hydrothermal method have the small size and good fluorescence properties. Compared with nitrate nitrogen and control, UDs (N content: 10 mg/L) could significantly inhibit the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa, with a growth inhibition rate of 78.6 %. Furthermore, UDs could also degrade MC-LR, achieving a degradation rate of 97.7 % after 96 h of illumination. The hetero-aggregation between UDs and algae on the surface of algal cells can block the absorption and utilization of light energy by algal cells, thereby reducing the synthesis of chlorophyll. UDs can also serve as a photocatalyst for the generation of free radicals (O2 −, ·OH), leading to cell membrane damage and inhibition of algal cell growth. Besides, O2 − and ·OH are capable of attacking the C4-C5 and C6-C7 bonds present in Adda-amino acids, subsequently facilitating the degradation of phycotoxins, ultimately resulting in their decomposition. These findings provide new insights into the application of urea-derived carbon dots in controlling harmful algal blooms and reducing MC-LR.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The uncontrolled reproduction of harmful algae and its toxic metabolites (microcystin, MC-LR) significantly inhibits aquatic animal growth, destroys the balance of aquatic ecosystems, and even threatens human health [1–3]. Therefore, its urgent need to control algal blooms on a globe scale. Currently, various physical, chemical, and biological technologies have been developed for controlling algal blooms. Certainly, the effectiveness of these methodologies in containing algal blooms is undeniable; however, they are accompanied by several salient issues [4]. Chemical agents, for instance, exhibit rapid and potent efficacy in disrupting cell membranes, yet they concurrently expedite the liberation of algal toxins into the aquatic realm, potentially exacerbating environmental hazards. Moreover, the application of these chemical agents may inadvertently compromise the integrity of the water environment [5]. On the other hand, physical technologies are inherently dependent on human intervention and specialized equipment, which could limit their widespread applicability and scalability [4,6]. Biotechnology, on the contrary, relies primarily on aquatic biota, necessitating extended periods for the desired outcomes to manifest, thereby adding to the temporal costs associated with its implementation [7]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop environmentally friendly methods that can simultaneously control algal bloom and algal toxin degradation.

Nanotechnology has significant advantages in controlling algal blooms and degrading MC-LR [8]. Ag nanomaterials (NMs) had a more significant inhibitory effect on algae growth [9]. TiO2 NMs can serve as a photocatalyst to generate free radicals (O2 −, ·OH), which attack algal cells and remove MC-LR under light conditions [10,11]. The growth effects of cyanobacteria aquatica and diatom brown algae after exposure to ZnO NMs [12]. However, despite the favorable algal inhibition effects demonstrated by these metal-based NMs, the extent of their potential environmental hazards, encompassing biotoxicity, bioaccumulation, and ecological repercussions, remains ambiguous [13]. Consequently, a certain gap still exists when these NMs are not environmentally friendly materials for inactivating algal cells and degrading MC-LR.

Carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of small size, high quantum yield, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and catalytic performance [14–16]. In particular, the wide absorption spectrum of CDs, covering the ultraviolet/visible spectrum range (from 300 nm) to the near-infrared spectrum range (800 nm), leading to CDs having broad application prospects in the photocatalysis [17–19]. Upon photoexcitation, CDs can generate electrons (e−) and holes (h+ ) (Eqs. (1), and these photogenerated carriers subsequently yield free radicals through the oxidation of water molecules or reduction of oxygen (Eqs. (2) and (3). These free radicals further react with pollutants to mediate the degradation of pollutants (Eqs. (4) and (5) [20,21].
(1) CDs + hv = e− + h+
(2) e− + O2 = O2 −
(3) H2O + h+ = ·OH + H+
(4) O2 − + pollutants → degrade products
(5) ·OH + pollutants → degrade products

For example, CDs (amine-rich) can efficiently harvest light energy to drive H2O2 photolysis and produce a quantity of ·OH for the remediation of aromatic hydrocarbon pollutants (Benzene, Ethylbenzene, Toluene and Xylene) and real wastewater [22,23]. Previous studies have shown that free radicals are also powerful tools for the inactivation of harmful algal cells and degradation of MC-LR [24]. Due to the strong toxic effects of MC-LR, which has been classified as a probable human carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer with further studies, a provisional guideline limit of 1 μg/L MC-LR has been set specifically for human drinking water [25]. Thus, CDs may be an ideal material for photocatalytic inactivation of algae and degradation of MC-LR.

Urea can be used as the raw material for preparing CDs due to its containing 20 % carbon (C) and 46 % nitrogen (N), which enables the successful doping of CDs with N [26,27]. However, whether urea-derived CDs (UDs) have good catalytic performance in generating free radicals to inhibit algal blooms and degrade MC-LR? It has been reported that CDs doped with N can improve the charge separation efficiency to enhance the photocatalytic performance of CDs [28,29]. Besides, C materials can heteroaggregate with algal cells in water environments, which will increase the intracellular ROS level and also affect the photosynthesis of cyanobacteria cells [30,31]. Therefore, we hypothesize that UDs can hetero-aggregate with algal cells, thereby reducing chlorophyll synthesis by diminishing light absorption. Meanwhile, under photoexcitation, UDs can generate free radicals (O2 − and ·OH), which could cause membrane damage, inhibit algal cell growth, and further degrade MC-LR. Consequently, the aim of this study is to investigate: (1) the effects of UDs on the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa; (2) the mechanism (e.g., hetero-aggregation and free radical generation of UDs as a photocatalytic material; (3) the degradation pathway of microcystin by UDs.

The UDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method using urea as carbon source. Briefly, 15 g of urea (Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, and the reaction was carried out for 4 h at 200 °C in a Teflon-sealed reaction kettle. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the UDs were obtained by freeze drying. The size and morphologies of UDs were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Jem-2100F, Japan). The nitrogen (N) elemental content of UDs were determined with an automated elemental analyzer (UNICUBE, Elementar, Germany). The chemical structure information was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence performance of UDs was characterized by employing a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of photocatalytic properties of UDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence lifetime of UDs was analyzed by using a steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK). The valence band (VB) edge of UDs was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Versaprobe III, Japan). Based on the UV data of UDs, the tac model is used to calculate the optical band gap value Eg of UDs. The tac model formula is: αhʋ = A (hʋ-Eg)n, where A, α, h and ʋ are the constant, absorption coefficient, planck constant and optical frequency, respectively, and n = 1/2 or 2 (direct and indirect semiconductors) is selected according to the material properties. The calculated (Ahʋ)2 and hʋ (incident photon energy) are plotted as tac plots, and the bandgap energy is determined by the extension of the intersection point between the linear part of the extrapolation plot and the incident photon energy axis [32,33]. In order to determine whether the UDs photocatalytic process occurred in low-concentration exposure experiments, the free radicals produced in algal media after exposure to UDs materials for 96 h were measured by electronic ESR (A300, Bruker, Germany). Including hole (h +), superoxide radical (O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (·OH). Using 300 W xenon lamp and filter larger than 420 nm to separate darkness and light for 10 min, using 100 μL 100 mM 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) aqueous solution as the trap agent to detect the ·OH generated, using 50 μL methanol, 100 μL 100 mM DMPO aqueous solution as the trap agent to detect O2 −. The h + was detected using 100 μL 20 mM 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyl-1-oxide (TEMPO) as a trap agent.

The model cyanobacteria (Microcystis aeruginosa, Microcystis, FACHB-315), which secretes microcystic toxins, was purchased from the freshwater algae culture bank of the Wuhan Institute of Hydrobiology, China. BG11 medium (pH 7.0) was used for culture. The composition of the BG11 medium was listed in Table. S1. The experimental conditions were: ambient temperature 25 ± 1 ℃, light 12 h (1620 Lm) and dark 12 h cycle culture.

In the high-concentration exposure experiment, the logarithmic growth phase of algal cells was collected and cultured in N-free medium (the medium composition was listed in Table. S2) for 5 days to deplete the N in the cells. The algal cells were inoculated in 250 mL conical bottle, the concentration of NaNO3 in normal medium was taken as the standard concentration of N source in the medium, and the algal cells were exposed with NO3 − (NaNO3, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), NH4 + (NH4Cl, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) and UDs materials with equal N content. The total algal fluid was 100 mL and the initial algal density was 1.0 × 106 cell/mL. The algal cell suspension was shaken every 12 h and sampled every 24 h. At 24, 48, 72, and 96 h exposure, the algal cell density was measured using a microscopic counting method.

In an environmentally relevant N concentration exposure experiment, BG11 was diluted 10-fold as a medium to simulate natural water bodies. Microcystis aeruginosa were placed in N-free medium with different concentrations of UDs, the total nitrogen content of the medium was 1, 5, 10 mg/L [34], and the N-free medium was used as the control. The UDs was pre-suspended in the medium for exposure experiment. The initial algal density was 1 × 106 cell/mL and the volume was 100 mL. The algal cell suspension was shaken every 12 h and sampled every 24 h. The algal cell density was determined by flow cytometry at 24, 48, 72 and 96 h exposure. The TaiHu Lake (Wuxi City, Jiangsu Province, China, N 31.48220°; E 120.27177°) water were prepared to verify the UDs inhibiting effect on algal blooms in natural water. Natural water was filtered with 0.45 μm filter membrane, sterilized at 121 °C for 30 min, and then cultured with algae seed. Indicators of natural water are shown in Table S3. The method for the measurement of water-soluble chlorophyll content and fat-soluble chlorophyll content in algal cells was shown in the Supplementary text S1. The levels of oxidative stress and membrane damage of Microcystis aeruginosa cells were determined by 2 ', 7 '-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCFH-DA) and iodipropionic acid (PI) staining. The specific determination method was shown in Supplementary text S2. For the determination of SOD and CAT enzyme activity, kits were purchased from Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Detailed methods for the determination of three-dimensional fluorescence spectra in the references was shown in Supplementary text S3.

Based on the environmentally relevant N concentration exposure experiment, algal cell suspension containing UDs was prepared in BG11 medium without N, and the density of Microcystis aeruginosa in the solution was 1 × 106 cells /mL. Samples of the UDs and algal cell suspensions were immediately taken and the absorbance of the suspensions at 680 nm for 2 h were measured using a microplate reader. The curves were mapped as co-settling curves. Settling curves of the UDs and Microcystis aeruginosa suspensions were individually determined using the same method. The settling curves of the UDs were then added to those of the algal cell suspensions to create sum-settling curves. By comparing the distance between the total settlement curve and the co-settlement curve (ΔODplateau), the heterogeneous aggregation of algae and UDs was determined. The state of algal cells was photographed using biological scanning electron microscopy (HITACHI, Japan). The specific processing method is shown in Supplementary text S4. The absorbance of algal cell suspension at 264 nm was determined by ultraviolet spectrophotometry. For the determination methods in the references was shown in Supplementary text S5. The specific determination method of electrolyte leakage rate of algal cells in the references was shown in Supplementary text S6.

The content of microcystin inside and outside algal cells was determined by ELISA kit protocols. For details was shown in Supplementary text S7 . Firstly, MC-LR (50 μg/L) was prepared and UDs (10 mg/L) was added. The contents of MC-LR after exposure to UDs for 96 h were detected by HPLC­MS/MS (Thermo Scientific, Germany). In order to determine which free radicals produced by UDs under light have the greatest influence on MC-LR degradation, ROS quenching experiments were carried out. Hydroxyl radical (⋅OH), hole (h +), and superoxide radical (O2 −) were quenched with sodium oxalate (0.5 mM), isopropyl alcohol (0.5 mM), and SOD enzyme (200 U/mL), respectively, and the content of MC-LR under each treatment was determined. The intermediate products of MC-LR degradation by UDs were measured by HPLC-MS/MS (Waters HSS T3 column, 2.1 × 100 mm × 1.8 μm). The sample solution (20 µL) was injected directly into the HPLC-MS/MS system (flow rate: 0.2 mL/min) to analyze the reaction intermediates in positive ion mode. The mobile phase consists of two components: A: deionized water; B: methanol containing 0.1 % formic acid (v/v). The elution gradient orders were: 0–0.5 min (80 % solution A + 20 % solution B), 0.5–10 min (5 % solution A + 95 % solution B), and 10–11 min (5 % solution A + 95 % solution B). The MC-LR standard solution was prepared by diluting the stock solution (10 mg/L) with a mixture of 20 % A and 80 % B before using [16].

Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation, and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Fisher LSD were performed using Origin Test and analyze the statistical significance between the treatments. The statistical significance level was P < 0.05 [35].

The UDs prepared by hydrothermal method using urea as the raw material were uniformly spherical with an average size of 3.4 nm and lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 a and 1b). UDs had a UV–vis absorption band at 270 nm (Fig. 1c) due to the π-π* transition within carbonized core [36]. UDs emitted blue fluorescence (Fig. 1c), and the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of UDs were 300 nm and 408 nm, respectively. These results suggested that UDs had the small size and fluorescent properties, similar as CDs [37]. As shown in the Fig. 1d, at 1450 cm−1, 2821 cm−1, 1582 cm−1, and 3038 cm−1, the UDs has − C − O, −C = O, −C − H and stretching vibrations, respectively [38]. The − N − H out-of-plane bending vibration and − N − H stretching vibration appeared at 810 cm−1 and 3188 cm−1 due to the role of urea as a N dopant. XPS further confirmed the presence of C, O, and N elements (Fig. 1e, 1f and 1 g, Fig. S2), as well as C-N, C = O, C = C, and C = N in the structural composition of UDs, which indicates that N was successfully retained in the structure of UDs [39]. Full XPS patterns and the O1s High-resolution XPS spectra peaks of UDs as shown in the Fig. S1 and Fig. S2. The N content contains in UDs was 4.1 % (Table. S3). N doping changed the local electronic structure of CDs and reduces the inner energy trap between sp2 domains to inhibit non-radiative quenching, which greatly enhances the binding between CDs and ions and increases the photoluminescence quantum yield of CDs [40,41]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of UDs is 3.1 ns (Fig. 1g), which was longer that of previous reported CDs (1.42 – 1.6 ns) [42,43]. The longer the fluorescence lifetime is, the better the carrier separation, the higher probability of photogenerated charge participating in the photocatalytic reaction, and the better photocatalysis [44]. Therefore, UDs had good electronic transfer ability, and could change its electronic structure, increased the absorbance of the material in the visible light region, and improved the generation of excited states through enhance the light capture of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of UDs on the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa cells
ce:section.ce:para: NO3 −-N, NH4 +-N, and UDs-N were used to explore the Microcystis aeruginosa growth response to UDs in BG11 medium (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 2 a, in the NO3 −-N treatment group, the algal cells began to grow explosively from 48 h, and the algal density exceeded 107 cell/mL, reaching the bloom standard [45]. While both NH4 + and UDs treatment groups had no significant effect on the growth and algal density of Microcystis aeruginosa within 96 h. Compared with CK, the growth rates of Microcystis aeruginosa under NO3 −-N, NH4 +-N and UDs-N treatments were 196.7 %, −36.5 % and −38.4 % after 96 h, respectively (Fig. 2b). These results showed that NO3 −-N was beneficial to the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa, the excessive reproduction of cyanobacteria cells will eventually lead to the occurrence of cyanobacteria blooms, while NH4 + and UDs treated with the same N content inhibited the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa cells. It was found that when exposing Microcystis cells, both toxic and non-toxic, to different N compounds (NO3 −-N, NH4 +-N, Urea-N), NO3 −-N promoted a higher growth rate in Microcystis aeruginosa cells than NH4 +-N [46]. The high concentration of NH4 +-N in the environment may cause algal cell poisoning and lead to a decrease in algal cell growth [47]. Even if the algal cells are NH4 +-N starved, the addition of NH4 +-N will suppress the algal cells' photosynthesis for several hours [48].

However, the UDs also inhibited the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa. The experiment found that exposure to microalgal cells with low, medium and high concentrations of CDs showed that the growth rate of microalgal cells gradually decreased with the increase of concentration, so it is not difficult to see that exposure to high concentration of CDs is also one of the causes of algal cell death [49]. Therefore, the effect of UDs on the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa cells at the level of environment-related N concentration was further investigated (Fig. S4). Compared with CK, the growth and density of Microcystis aeruginosa cells significantly decreased under UDs (N content 1, 5, and 10 mg/L, respectively) treatment within 96 h (Fig. 2c). The inhibition rates of the three treatment groups were 63.3 %, 75.2 % and 78.6 %, respectively (Fig. 2d). Moreover, the UDs in natural water also significantly reduced the growth rate of algal cells within 96 h (Fig. S5). These results suggest that UDs inhibit the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa cells, even at environmentally relevant N levels and in natural settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inhibitory mechanism of UDs on algal growth
ce:section.ce:para: As autotrophs, Microcystis aeruginosa can use light energy through photosynthesis to produce the energy [50]. In particular, photosynthesis of algal cells is not only affected by chlorophyll, but also related to phycobilin, a photosynthetic auxiliary pigment, so the content of both can be used to measure the physiological health of algae and to evaluate the photosynthetic intensity of algal cells [51,52,53]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with CK, the contents of fat-soluble chlorophyll (chlorophyll a (Chl a) and beta-carotene (Car)) and phycobilin (phycoerythrin (PE), phycocyanin (PC) and allophycocyanin (APC)) decreased by 73.3 %, 74.2 %, 72.5 %, 79.5 % and 78.5 % after UDs (10 mg/L) treatment, respectively. UDs reduced the content of chlorophyll and phycobilin in Microcystis aeruginosa cells, resulting in low light capture, which limited energy supply for algal cell growth, leading to inhibiting algal cell expansion. Besides, the oxidative stress level of algal cells after UDs treatment is significantly increased. Specifically, the ROS level of Microcystis aeruginosa cells after UDs treatment was 52 % higher than that of the control group, and the cell membrane was seriously damaged (Fig. 3b, Fig. S6). Malondialdehyde (MDA) was significantly increased in algal cells after exposure to UDs (Fig. S7), reflecting an enhanced lipid peroxidation level in Microcystis aeruginosa. Meanwhile, both ROS levels and membrane damage (PI level) in algal cells were notably increased upon exposure to UDs (Fig. S6, Fig. S8a, and Fig. S8b). Compared with CK,the activities of both SOD (Fig. S8c) and CAT (Fig. S8d) enzymes were heightened in the UDs treatment group. Specifically, in the UDs treatment group with an N content of 10 mg/L, the activities of SOD and CAT enzymes increased significantly by 60 % and 98.6 %, respectively, compared to the CK group. The SOD enzyme serves as the primary defense against ROS, breaking down O2 − into H2O2 and O2. Subsequently, CAT converts the H2O2 into water and O2, thereby mitigating oxidative damage to the cells [54]. Furthermore, UDs treatment significantly increased the content of organic matter in the medium. As shown in Fig. S9. the contents of extracellular tyrosine proteins (Regional Ⅰ), tryptophan proteins (Regional Ⅱ), fulvic acids (Regional Ⅲ), and humic acids (Regional Ⅴ) were increased under UDs exposure. Studies have shown that microalgae can secrete fulvic acid as a C source to maintain their growth under external stimulation [55]. Jiao et al. found that algal cells secrete humic acid to alleviate the harm of small-size microplastics to cells [56]. Therefore, these results indicated that UDs exposure induced the membrane damage and an increase ROS levels in algal cells.

Besides, studies found that, compared with graphene, the aggregation degree was improved along with the time between the graphene quantum dots and algal cells, which could increase the damage degree of the algal cell membrane [57]. As shown in Fig. S10, there was significantly hetero-aggregation between UDs and algal cells based on the co– and sum-setting curves. The SEM images also demonstrated that there was obviously heterogeneous aggregation between algal cells and UDs (Fig. 3c). The UDs attached to the cell surface may cause shading effects, reducing algal cell light capture and/or reducing cell transport or absorption of substances through the cell membrane [58]. Additionally, CDs-modified composite material could generate O2 −, h+ , and ·OH, and inactivating E.coli under visible light irradiation [59]. Similar to CDs, UDs can also absorb ultraviolet light (Fig. 1c), which suggests that they may enter the photoexcited state and generate electron-hole pairs through ultraviolet excitation [60,61]. As shown in Fig. 3d, compared with CK, the signal intensity of ·OH and O2 − were significantly increased after exposure to UDs, indicating that it increases the production of free radicals in the medium. While the signal intensity of h + is opposite (Fig. 3d), the higher the peak signal intensity, the lower the h + generation, indicating that h + generation also increases after exposure to UDs. The valence band (VB) and bandgap energy of the UDs are 1.0 ev and 3.6 ev (Fig. S11, and Fig. S12), respectively. As a result, the conduction band (CB) of the UDs is 2.6 ev. Photoactivated electrons can reduce oxygen to form photogenic holes, and then form hydrogen peroxide and produce hydroxyl radical through hv decomposition [62,63]. Therefore, UDs can generate electron-hole pairs under the excitation of light energy, subsequently generating free radicals that induce damage to algal cells and inhibit algal blooms.

However, in this study, UDs cannot produce ·OH through the oxidation of water molecules, but can produce free radicals (O2 − and ·OH) by initiating the reduction path of oxygen molecules [64]. The generated ·OH radicals are mainly derived from the reduction of O2 − by reacting with electrons, and O2 − originates from the reduction of O2 by electrons (Fig. 4 ). Therefore, the photogenerated electrons and holes from UDs reduce O2 in water, generating O2 − and ·OH that attack algal cell membranes, resulting in membrane damage and inhibiting the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa. Taken together, the hetero-aggregation between Microcystis aeruginosa and UDs blocked the absorption of light energy by algal cells, decreased the chlorophyll content of Microcystis aeruginosa, thereby reducing the ability of algal cells to use light energy and photosynthesis and inhibiting the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa. In addition, UDs can act as a photocatalytic material to generate free radicals (O2 − and ·OH) that attack algal cells, which improved levels of ROS and lipid peroxidation, and aggravated algal cell membrane damage, and ultimately, algal cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of microcystin by UDs
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to CK, the OD264 of Microcystis aeruginosa exhibited an increase following UDs treatment (Fig. 5 a), suggesting that UDs may enhance the membrane permeability of the algae. This hypothesis was further supported by the elevated electrolyte leakage rate (EL) observed, confirming that UDs indeed increased the membrane permeability of algal cells (Fig. S13). This finding was in line with the increased MDA content within algal cells and the elevated organic matter content in the medium (Fig. S7 and Fig. S9). Collectively, these results indicated that UDs treatment led to an increase in the membrane permeability of Microcystis aeruginosa, causing the leakage of both organic matter and electrolytes.Therefore, it is more conducive to release MC-LR from intracellular to extracellular. As shown in Fig. 5b, the levels of extracellular and intracellular MC-LR increased significantly after 96 h of UDs exposure. Consequently, following exposure to UDs, the elevated ROS levels in algal cells led to a significant impairment in the membrane permeability of Microcystis aeruginosa (Fig. 3b), culminating in the leakage of MC-LR toxin from within the algal cells to the external environment. UDs has been demostrated to produce free radicals as a photocatalyst (Fig. 3d and Fig. 4). Therefore, UDs can also degrade MC-LR excreted by algal cells during the photocatalysis process. As shown in Fig. S14, the degradation rate of MC-LR reached 97.7 % under co-exposure with UDs after 96 h illumination, which was better than that of dark condition. Comparing the content of microcystin after quenching free radicals, it was found that the ·OH played a major role in the degradation process (Fig. 5c). In the presence of O2 −, ·OH, and h + scavenger, the degradation of MC-LR by UDs were decreased as compared with that without scavenger, respectively. Surprisingly, after 96 h of exposure, the content of MC-LR in natural water treated with UDs decreased by 27.5 % compared to the CK (Fig. S15). These findings suggest that UDs have the potential to inhibit the growth of algal cells and decompose microcystin in natural water.

Urea-derived carbon dots (UDs) can significantly inhibit the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa and degrade MC-LR. UDs could decrease the light absorption of algal cells through hetero-aggregation, reduce the synthesis of chlorophyll, and generate free radicals (O2 −, ·OH), which induced cell membrane damage through photocatalysis. The damage of the cell membrane increased the permeability, leading to increasing the content of extracellular MC-LR significantly. Free radicals (O2 −, ·OH) produced by UDs under illumination can degrade MC-LR by attacking conjugated double bonds (C4-C5, C6-C7) in MC-LR, in which ·OH has the highest contribution in degradation of MC-LR. Overall, UDs can inhibit the growth of Microcystis aeruginosa after entering the water environment, and in the process, it can also degrade microcystin, a toxic by-product produced by algae, through photocatalysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42192572 and 42322705), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20230044 and BK20243048), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP622019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.130935.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as solid-state electron mediator and electron acceptor in S-scheme heterojunction for boosted photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
